violations.detail: Cards 1 ('Why is stack faster?') and 2 ('Why is heap slower?') are the same fact from two angles. Stack is fast because it's contiguous/no fragmentation; heap is slow because of fragmentation. Knowing one trivially gives you the other — classic sacrifice/gain mirror pair. Must merge or drop one.

Back reads 'The variable's enclosing scope (pushed on function call, popped on return)'. The parenthetical adds two new mechanical facts (when pushed, when popped) — this is not an abbreviation. Must be split or trimmed.

Back reads 'They are not tied to scope — they persist until explicitly freed or garbage collected'. The dash joins two distinct facts: (1) not tied to scope, (2) persist until freed/GC'd. Rule: every '—' in a back is a bug. Must be split.

violations.rule: Back brevity — semicolon joins two facts
violations.detail: Back reads 'The stack requires sizes known at compile time; the heap supports runtime-determined sizes'. Semicolon joins two independent facts. Rule: every ';' in a back is a bug. Must be split.

Card reads 'Stack size is typically {{c1::1-8 MB}}, while heap size is {{c2::virtually unlimited}}.' Each fill is an independent fact (stack size vs. heap size). This is a 2-item cloze enumeration — the exact pattern SKILL.md prohibits. Must split into separate cards.

Card reads 'Stack allocation is managed by the {{c1::compiler}}; heap allocation is managed by the {{c2::programmer or garbage collector}}.' Two violations: (1) semicolon joins two independent facts, (2) c1 and c2 are independent facts in cloze clothing — 2-item enumeration. Must split into separate cards.

violations.rule: Specific constant values without understanding why
violations.detail: Testing '1-8 MB' is a specific constant value with no companion card explaining WHY the stack is limited to this range. Card selection guide says to skip specific constant values without understanding why they matter.

Card 9 tests 'who frees heap memory' (programmer), and Card 10's c2 fill is 'programmer or garbage collector' for heap management. Significant overlap — the same core fact (programmer manages heap) tested twice.
